content:
{
  "lens": "architecture",
  "findings": [
    {
      "severity": "medium",
      "category": "coupling",
      "file": "src/auth/auth.controller.ts",
      "line": 1,
      "title": "Business logic embedded in HTTP controller",
      "description": "The controller contains authorization logic (token comparison on line 53), user existence checking, and orchestrates the full auth flow. This makes the business logic untestable without spinning up Express. The AuthService is already separated, but the controller does too much orchestration.",
      "suggestion": "Move the full login/register orchestration into AuthService methods that return result objects. The controller should only handle HTTP concerns (parse request, call service, format response).",
      "confidence": 0.82
    },
    {
      "severity": "low",
      "category": "data-exposure",
      "file": "src/auth/auth.model.ts",
      "line": 14,
      "title": "Model queries return password hash to all callers",
      "description": "findByEmail uses SELECT * which returns the passwordHash field to every caller. Only verifyPassword needs the hash, but every caller receives it, increasing the attack surface for accidental exposure (e.g., in logs, error messages, or API responses).",
      "suggestion": "Use explicit column selection: SELECT id, email, name FROM users for general queries. Create a separate findByEmailWithHash for authentication only.",
      "confidence": 0.77
    }
  ]
}
